hookPrompts.question-based: Create a compelling question-based hook that immediately engages the audience through thought-provoking inquiries. TECHNIQUES TO USE: (1) Assumption-challenging questions that make people reconsider their beliefs (2) Gap-revealing questions that expose what they don't know (3) Consequence questions that explore 'what if' scenarios (4) Personal reflection questions that make them examine their own experience (5) Paradox questions that present contradictory ideas. QUESTION FORMULAS: 'What if I told you that [common belief] is completely wrong?' / 'Have you ever wondered why [phenomenon] happens?' / 'What would happen if you [hypothetical scenario]?' / 'Why do [group] always [behavior] while [other group] [opposite behavior]?' / 'What's the one question that [expert/industry] never wants you to ask?' ADVANCED STRATEGIES: Start with a simple question then layer complexity, use rhetorical questions followed by direct questions, create question sequences that build momentum, mix obvious questions with surprising ones. PSYCHOLOGY: Questions activate the brain's search function and create open loops that demand answers. They also make content feel interactive and personalized. AVOID: Questions that are too easy/obvious, questions with simple yes/no answers, questions unrelated to your main topic, or too many questions in a row. PLATFORM ADAPTATIONS: For social media, use shorter, punchier questions. For long-form content, develop question narratives. For video, pause after questions for emphasis.
hookPrompts.shock-surprise: Create a shock and surprise hook that immediately jolts the audience with unexpected, counterintuitive, or mind-blowing information. SHOCK TECHNIQUES: (1) Counterintuitive facts that contradict common knowledge (2) Hidden truths about familiar things (3) Unexpected connections between unrelated concepts (4) Dramatic statistics that reframe perspective (5) Behind-the-scenes revelations (6) Future predictions that sound impossible (7) Historical facts that seem fake. SURPRISE FORMULAS: 'You'll never believe what [common thing] actually does to your [area of life]' / 'The [industry] has been hiding this from you for [time period]' / '[Number]% of people don't know that [shocking fact]' / 'Scientists just discovered that [belief] has been backwards this whole time' / 'This [simple thing] is secretly [dramatic impact]' SHOCK AMPLIFIERS: Use power words (secretly, hidden, never, impossible, shocking), include specific numbers/percentages, reference authoritative sources, create before/after mental images. CREDIBILITY MAINTENANCE: Always follow shock with evidence, cite sources when possible, explain the mechanism behind the surprise, address skepticism proactively. PSYCHOLOGY: Shock triggers the brain's threat detection system, creating instant attention and memorable moments. Surprise activates dopamine, making content more engaging and shareable. AVOID: False or exaggerated claims, shock for shock's sake without relevance, overusing superlatives, or breaking trust with misleading information. TEST FOR AUTHENTICITY: If your fact doesn't make you personally surprised, find a more shocking angle.
hookPrompts.controversial-polarizing: Create a controversial or polarizing hook that takes a bold, debatable stance designed to spark discussion and strong reactions. CONTROVERSY TYPES: (1) Challenging popular opinions (2) Defending unpopular positions (3) Exposing industry secrets (4) Questioning authority/experts (5) Reversing conventional wisdom (6) Taking sides in ongoing debates (7) Calling out hypocrisy. POLARIZING FORMULAS: 'Unpopular opinion: [controversial statement]' / '[Popular thing] is actually [negative judgment] and here's why' / 'I'm going to say what everyone's thinking but afraid to say about [topic]' / 'Hot take: [industry/group] is completely wrong about [belief]' / 'Why [popular practice] is destroying [outcome] (and what to do instead)' ESCALATION TECHNIQUES: Start with a moderate controversial statement, then escalate with evidence; present the minority viewpoint as if it's obvious; challenge the audience's core beliefs directly; use 'us vs them' language strategically. SAFETY STRATEGIES: Focus on ideas/practices, not personal attacks; back controversial claims with logic/evidence; acknowledge the opposition's perspective before dismantling it; show respect for people while challenging concepts. PSYCHOLOGY: Controversy activates tribal instincts and emotional investment. People share content they strongly agree or disagree with. ENGAGEMENT DRIVERS: Ask for opinions in comments, acknowledge this will be divisive, predict the backlash you'll receive. AVOID: Controversy about protected groups, offensive language, false dichotomies, or taking controversial stances you can't defend. RESPONSIBILITY: Ensure your controversial position adds value and isn't just inflammatory.
hookPrompts.emotional-appeal: Create an emotionally resonant hook that deeply connects with the audience's feelings, experiences, and human needs. EMOTIONAL CATEGORIES: (1) Nostalgia - childhood memories, simpler times, past experiences (2) Hope - future possibilities, dreams, optimism (3) Fear - loss, failure, missing out (4) Anger - injustice, frustration, betrayal (5) Joy - celebration, achievement, happiness (6) Sadness - loss, disappointment, empathy (7) Pride - accomplishment, identity, belonging (8) Love - connection, family, relationships. EMOTIONAL TRIGGERS: Sensory details that recreate experiences, personal stories that mirror audience struggles, universal human experiences, milestone moments (birth, death, graduation), underdog stories, sacrifice narratives, triumph over adversity. LANGUAGE PATTERNS: Use 'you' to make it personal, employ sensory words (warm, bright, heavy, smooth), include emotion words (heartbroken, elated, terrified, peaceful), create vivid scenes they can visualize, use metaphors that evoke feelings. EMOTIONAL ESCALATION: Start with a relatable feeling, build intensity through details, reach an emotional peak, then transition to your main message. AUTHENTICITY MARKERS: Share personal vulnerability, admit mistakes or struggles, show real consequences of emotions, avoid manipulative language. PSYCHOLOGY: Emotions drive decision-making more than logic. Emotional content is remembered longer and shared more frequently. People connect with brands/creators who understand their feelings. PLATFORM STRATEGIES: Video allows for tone and facial expressions, written content needs more descriptive language, social media requires immediate emotional impact. AVOID: Manipulating trauma for views, fake emotional stories, overwhelming negative emotions without resolution, or trivializing serious emotional experiences.
hookPrompts.relatable-humor: Create a humorous and relatable hook that uses comedy, shared experiences, and everyday situations to instantly connect with your audience. HUMOR TYPES: (1) Observational - funny things about daily life (2) Self-deprecating - making fun of yourself (3) Situational - funny scenarios everyone faces (4) Exaggeration - taking normal things to extremes (5) Incongruity - unexpected combinations (6) Callbacks - referencing shared cultural moments (7) Timing - perfectly timed reveals or punchlines. RELATABILITY SOURCES: Common frustrations (slow Wi-Fi, autocorrect fails, waiting in line), universal experiences (Monday mornings, diet attempts, procrastination), generational moments (dial-up internet, VHS tapes), relationship dynamics (family dinners, friend groups), work situations (meetings, emails, deadlines), modern life absurdities (social media behavior, technology dependence). COMEDY FORMULAS: 'You know you're [age/type] when [relatable behavior]' / 'Me: [intention] Also me: [contradictory action]' / 'That awkward moment when [universal experience]' / 'Nobody: Absolutely nobody: Me: [weird behavior]' / 'Expectation vs Reality: [contrast]' TIMING TECHNIQUES: Build up tension then release with punchline, use the rule of three (setup, reinforce, punchline), pause for effect in video content, use unexpected word choices for surprise. VISUAL HUMOR: Describe funny facial expressions, use exaggerated body language, create mental images of ridiculous scenarios. PSYCHOLOGY: Humor releases endorphins and creates positive associations with your content. Shared laughter builds community and trust. People share funny content to entertain others and appear witty themselves. AUDIENCE TARGETING: Reference age-specific experiences, cultural touchstones, professional inside jokes, geographic humor. AVOID: Offensive stereotypes, humor that punches down, inside jokes your audience won't understand, or trying too hard to be funny. AUTHENTICITY: Use your natural humor style, share genuinely funny personal experiences, don't force jokes that don't land naturally.
hookPrompts.pov: Create a personal point-of-view hook that shares unique perspectives, personal insights, and individual experiences that offer fresh takes on familiar topics. POV CATEGORIES: (1) Industry insider perspective (2) Generational viewpoint (3) Cultural background lens (4) Personal transformation story (5) Contrarian individual view (6) Expert practitioner angle (7) Outsider looking in (8) Lived experience authority. PERSPECTIVE FRAMEWORKS: 'As someone who [credential/experience], I've noticed that [observation]' / 'After [time period] of [activity], here's what nobody tells you about [topic]' / 'Everyone talks about [popular view], but from my experience [different view]' / 'I used to believe [old belief] until [experience] taught me [new insight]' / 'Working in [industry] for [time] has shown me that [revelation]' AUTHENTICITY MARKERS: Specific personal details, admission of past mistakes, evolution of thinking over time, concrete examples from your life, vulnerable moments of uncertainty, lessons learned through failure. UNIQUE ANGLE STRATEGIES: Combine two unexpected perspectives, challenge your own industry from inside, apply lessons from one field to another, share the view from a different life stage, reveal what outsiders get wrong about your world. CREDIBILITY BUILDERS: Mention specific experiences, reference time invested, acknowledge limitations of your perspective, cite personal mistakes as learning moments, show ongoing growth/learning. STORYTELLING ELEMENTS: Set the scene of your experience, introduce conflict or challenge you faced, show your thought process and evolution, reveal what you learned or how you changed, connect to broader implications. PSYCHOLOGY: Personal perspectives feel more trustworthy than generic advice. Audiences connect with individual journeys and authentic voices. POV content feels exclusive and insider-ish. AVOID: Presenting opinion as universal fact, humble-bragging disguised as insight, perspectives without supporting experience, or claiming expertise you don't have. ENGAGEMENT: Invite others to share their POV, acknowledge different experiences exist, ask if others have noticed similar patterns.
hookPrompts.trend-based: Create a trend-based hook that connects your content to current events, viral phenomena, popular culture, or emerging movements that your audience is already engaged with. TREND CATEGORIES: (1) Social media trends (challenges, memes, viral formats) (2) News events (current affairs, breaking stories) (3) Cultural moments (award shows, holidays, seasons) (4) Tech developments (new apps, features, innovations) (5) Pop culture (movies, shows, celebrity news) (6) Industry shifts (market changes, new regulations) (7) Generational movements (social causes, lifestyle changes). CONNECTION STRATEGIES: Direct application ('Here's how [trend] applies to [your topic]'), contrarian take ('Why [trend] is actually about [your topic]'), prediction ('What [trend] means for the future of [your topic]'), lessons learned ('What [trend] teaches us about [your topic]'), behind-the-scenes ('The real reason [trend] happened relates to [your topic]'). TREND FORMULAS: '[Trending topic] just proved what I've been saying about [your topic]' / 'Everyone's talking about [trend], but here's what it really means for [audience]' / 'The [trend] controversy reveals something important about [topic]' / 'Why [trend] is a perfect example of [your message]' / 'What [trend] gets right (and wrong) about [topic]' TIMING STRATEGIES: Jump on trends early but not too early, reference the trend at its peak awareness, create content that will age well even when trend fades, batch similar trend content for consistent posting. AUTHENTICITY MEASURES: Only use trends you genuinely understand, connect trends meaningfully to your expertise, avoid forced connections, maintain your brand voice even when trend-jacking. PLATFORM OPTIMIZATION: Use trending hashtags strategically, reference current memes or formats, adapt trend content to each platform's culture, engage with trend conversations authentically. PSYCHOLOGY: Trend-based content feels timely and relevant. People want to engage with current conversations. Trending topics have built-in audience interest and search volume. LONGEVITY PLANNING: Create evergreen versions of trend content, extract universal principles from specific trends, build trend awareness into content calendar. AVOID: Offensive or inappropriate trend connections, jumping on every trend regardless of relevance, being late to trends that have peaked, or making tone-deaf connections to serious events.
hookPrompts.challenge-dare: Create a challenge or dare hook that motivates your audience to take specific action, test themselves, or step outside their comfort zone in service of growth or discovery. CHALLENGE TYPES: (1) Skill-building challenges (30-day improvements) (2) Mindset shifts (perspective experiments) (3) Behavior changes (habit modifications) (4) Creative challenges (artistic or innovative tasks) (5) Physical challenges (fitness or health goals) (6) Social challenges (relationship or communication) (7) Professional challenges (career development) (8) Awareness challenges (observation exercises). MOTIVATION PSYCHOLOGY: Appeal to competitive instincts, promise specific outcomes, create social accountability, offer recognition for completion, design achievable but stretch goals, provide clear success metrics. CHALLENGE FORMULAS: 'I dare you to [specific action] for [timeframe] and see what happens' / 'Try this [simple action] and watch how it changes [outcome]' / 'Can you go [timeframe] without [common behavior]? Here's why you should try' / 'The [number]-day [action] challenge that will transform your [area of life]' / 'I challenge you to [action] - but only if you're ready for [benefit]' FRAMEWORK DESIGN: Clear rules and boundaries, specific duration, measurable outcomes, built-in checkpoints, community element, escalating difficulty, celebration of completion. ENGAGEMENT AMPLIFIERS: Ask for commitment in comments, create hashtags for sharing, provide progress tracking tools, share your own attempt at the challenge, highlight participant successes. PSYCHOLOGY DRIVERS: Challenges activate goal-setting behavior, public commitment increases follow-through, competition motivates action, clear timeframes create urgency, social proof encourages participation. DIFFICULTY CALIBRATION: Make it challenging enough to feel meaningful, achievable enough to build confidence, specific enough to avoid confusion, flexible enough for different skill levels. SUPPORT SYSTEMS: Provide resources for success, address common obstacles, create community spaces, offer modifications for different situations, celebrate small wins along the way. AVOID: Impossible or dangerous challenges, vague or unmeasurable goals, challenges without clear purpose, peer pressure tactics, or challenges that could harm certain individuals. FOLLOW-UP: Plan content around challenge results, share participant stories, analyze lessons learned, create advanced versions for completers.
hookPrompts.transformation: Create a transformation or before/after hook that showcases dramatic change, improvement, or results to inspire and motivate your audience. TRANSFORMATION TYPES: (1) Physical changes (fitness, health, appearance) (2) Mental/emotional growth (confidence, mindset, habits) (3) Skill development (abilities, expertise, performance) (4) Financial improvement (income, savings, investments) (5) Relationship enhancement (communication, connection, boundaries) (6) Career advancement (promotions, transitions, success) (7) Lifestyle changes (location, schedule, priorities) (8) Creative evolution (artistic growth, innovation). STORYTELLING STRUCTURE: Establish the 'before' state with specific details, identify the catalyst or turning point, describe the journey and challenges faced, reveal the 'after' state with concrete evidence, extract the lessons and principles that enabled change. CONTRAST AMPLIFICATION: Use specific numbers and metrics, include timeframes for context, show visual or measurable proof, describe emotional states before and after, highlight what others said about the change, compare old vs new behaviors/thinking. TRANSFORMATION FORMULAS: '[Timeframe] ago I was [before state]. Today I [after state]. Here's exactly how' / 'From [negative state] to [positive state]: The [number] things that changed everything' / 'How I went from [starting point] to [end point] in [timeframe]' / 'The transformation that surprised everyone (including me)' / 'Before: [old reality]. After: [new reality]. The difference? [key insight]' CREDIBILITY ELEMENTS: Include specific dates and timelines, show receipts or proof when possible, acknowledge setbacks and failures, credit helpers and influences, explain the process honestly, share what didn't work. INSPIRATION STRATEGIES: Make the transformation feel achievable, break down the steps taken, address common obstacles, show that ordinary people can achieve extraordinary results, emphasize mindset shifts over just tactics. PSYCHOLOGY: Transformation stories activate mirror neurons and make change feel possible. Before/after creates powerful mental contrasts. People are naturally drawn to success stories and improvement narratives. EMOTIONAL JOURNEY: Capture the frustration of the 'before', the hope during change, the excitement of breakthrough moments, the confidence of the 'after'. AVOID: Unrealistic timelines, overnight success stories, omitting the struggle, taking all credit, or promising identical results for everyone. ACTIONABILITY: Extract specific principles others can apply, identify the mindset shifts required, provide starting point recommendations, address common misconceptions about change.
hookPrompts.storytelling: Create a compelling narrative hook that draws the audience into an engaging story, anecdote, or scenario that perfectly sets up your main content. STORY TYPES: (1) Personal experience narratives (2) Client/customer success stories (3) Historical anecdotes (4) Hypothetical scenarios (5) Case study stories (6) Origin stories (7) Failure and recovery narratives (8) Day-in-the-life stories (9) Conflict and resolution tales. NARRATIVE ELEMENTS: Strong opening that establishes setting and character, clear conflict or tension that needs resolution, specific details that make the story vivid and believable, emotional stakes that make the audience care, turning point or revelation moment, satisfying resolution that connects to your message. STORYTELLING FORMULAS: 'It was [time/setting] when [character] faced [problem]...' / 'Everyone said [character] was crazy when they [action], but [outcome]...' / 'Picture this: [vivid scene description] when suddenly [unexpected event]...' / 'Three years ago, [character] made a decision that would [impact]. Here's what happened...' / 'The [time] I learned [lesson] the hard way...' ENGAGEMENT TECHNIQUES: Start in the middle of action (in medias res), use sensory details to create immersion, include dialogue to bring characters to life, create cliffhangers and tension, use pacing to control emotional intensity, end with clear connection to your main point. CHARACTER DEVELOPMENT: Make characters relatable and three-dimensional, show their motivations and fears, include character flaws and vulnerabilities, demonstrate growth and change, use specific names and details when appropriate. UNIVERSAL THEMES: David vs Goliath (underdog stories), man vs nature (overcoming obstacles), growth and learning, love and loss, triumph over adversity, justice and injustice, sacrifice and reward. PSYCHOLOGY: Stories activate multiple brain regions and create emotional investment. Narratives are how humans naturally process and remember information. Stories create empathy and connection between teller and audience. PLATFORM ADAPTATION: Shorter stories for social media with quick payoffs, longer narratives for blogs and videos, episodic storytelling for series content, visual storytelling for image-heavy platforms. AVOID: Stories that are too long for the platform, narratives without clear connection to your message, fictional stories presented as real, overly complex plots that confuse, or stories that don't respect audience time. AUTHENTICITY: Use real experiences when possible, protect others' privacy appropriately, acknowledge when stories are hypothetical, maintain emotional honesty even in dramatic retellings.
hookPrompts.list-countdown: Create an engaging list or countdown hook that organizes valuable information into numbered, digestible segments that promise specific value. LIST TYPES: (1) Tips and strategies (2) Mistakes to avoid (3) Tools and resources (4) Signs and symptoms (5) Steps and processes (6) Examples and case studies (7) Reasons and benefits (8) Secrets and insights (9) Facts and statistics (10) Predictions and trends. NUMBER PSYCHOLOGY: Odd numbers feel more authentic (3, 5, 7), larger numbers suggest comprehensive coverage (21, 50, 100), smaller numbers feel achievable (3, 5), round numbers are easy to remember (10, 20, 50), specific numbers create curiosity (17, 23, 43). LIST FORMULAS: '[Number] [item type] that will [benefit/outcome]' / 'The top [number] [mistakes/secrets/tips] that [expert group] don't want you to know' / '[Number] signs you're [status/condition]' / '[Number] things I wish I knew before [milestone/event]' / 'Here are [number] ways to [achieve goal] without [common sacrifice]' STRUCTURE STRATEGIES: Start with most important/surprising item, build momentum with increasingly valuable items, save best for last, group related items together, use parallel structure for consistency, include brief explanations for each item. ENGAGEMENT BOOSTERS: Promise specific outcomes for each item, use action-oriented language, include personal examples or stories for items, ask audience which item resonates most, encourage saving/bookmarking for reference. VALUE PROPOSITION: Make each item actionable and specific, avoid filler content just to reach number, ensure items are genuinely helpful, provide enough detail to be useful, balance comprehensiveness with clarity. PREVIEW STRATEGY: Tease the most compelling items in your hook, hint at surprising revelations to come, promise specific types of value, create anticipation for the full list. PSYCHOLOGY: Lists satisfy our desire for organization and completion, numbered items feel more authoritative and comprehensive, countdowns create anticipation and momentum, lists are highly shareable and saveable. PLATFORM OPTIMIZATION: Use visual formatting for readability, break longer lists into carousel posts, create quote cards for best items, thread lists across multiple social posts, design infographics for complex lists. AVOID: Random ordering without logic, items that overlap significantly, lists that are too long for platform, vague or generic list items, or promising numbers you can't deliver meaningfully. FOLLOW-UP CONTENT: Deep dive into individual list items, create expanded versions, ask audience for their additions to the list, turn lists into series content.
hookPrompts.how-to-tutorial: Create a how-to or tutorial hook that promises to teach your audience specific, actionable skills or knowledge they can immediately apply. TUTORIAL TYPES: (1) Step-by-step processes (2) Skill-building lessons (3) Problem-solving guides (4) Tool tutorials (5) Technique demonstrations (6) Strategy implementations (7) Creative projects (8) Troubleshooting guides (9) Optimization methods (10) Learning frameworks. EDUCATIONAL FRAMEWORKS: Promise specific outcomes, break complex tasks into simple steps, provide clear success criteria, anticipate common questions, address prerequisites upfront, include troubleshooting tips, offer multiple difficulty levels. TUTORIAL FORMULAS: 'How to [achieve specific outcome] in [timeframe] (even if [common obstacle])' / 'The complete beginner's guide to [skill/topic]' / '[Number]-step system to [result] without [common pain point]' / 'Learn [skill] in [timeframe]: The method [expert group] use' / 'How to [goal] (the right way)' CREDIBILITY BUILDERS: Share your qualifications to teach, mention years of experience, reference successful students/clients, show your own results, cite sources and methods, acknowledge limitations of approach. LEARNING PSYCHOLOGY: Adults learn better with immediate application, clear benefits, and bite-sized steps. People prefer learning from those slightly ahead of them rather than distant experts. Practical tutorials feel more valuable than theoretical knowledge. ENGAGEMENT STRATEGIES: Encourage following along in real-time, ask for questions and clarifications, provide templates or worksheets, create before/after opportunities, build in practice moments. ACCESSIBILITY FEATURES: Define technical terms, provide alternative methods, accommodate different learning styles, offer different paces, include visual aids and examples, address common mistakes proactively. COMPLETION MOTIVATION: Set realistic timeframes, celebrate small wins, provide progress markers, offer additional resources, create community for learners, follow up with advanced tutorials. PLATFORM ADAPTATION: Video tutorials for complex visual processes, written guides for reference materials, interactive tutorials for hands-on learning, audio tutorials for multitasking learners. AVOID: Skipping foundational steps, using jargon without explanation, making assumptions about prior knowledge, rushing through complex concepts, or promising unrealistic timeframes. SUPPORT SYSTEMS: Provide additional resources, create FAQ sections, offer help channels, share troubleshooting guides, connect learners with each other, update content based on feedback.
hookPrompts.problem-solution: Create a problem-solution hook that identifies specific pain points your audience faces and positions your content as the answer they've been searching for. PROBLEM IDENTIFICATION: Universal frustrations (time management, decision fatigue), industry-specific challenges (marketing attribution, team communication), demographic pain points (first-time parents, career changers), seasonal problems (holiday stress, summer planning), emerging issues (remote work challenges, technology overwhelm). PROBLEM ARTICULATION: Use specific language that shows deep understanding, include emotional impact of the problem, mention failed attempts at solutions, reference time or money costs, acknowledge the complexity and nuance, validate the frustration and struggle. SOLUTION FRAMEWORKS: Root cause solutions (addressing underlying issues), systematic approaches (step-by-step processes), mindset shifts (changing perspective), tool recommendations (specific resources), behavior modifications (habit changes), strategic thinking (new approaches). HOOK FORMULAS: 'Struggling with [specific problem]? Here's the [solution type] that finally worked' / 'If you're tired of [frustration], this [solution] will change everything' / 'The real reason you're [struggling] (and what to do about it)' / 'Stop [ineffective behavior]. Start [effective alternative]' / 'Finally, a solution to [problem] that actually works' CREDIBILITY ESTABLISHMENT: Share personal experience with the problem, reference others you've helped, cite research or data supporting your solution, acknowledge what doesn't work and why, demonstrate understanding of complexity. SOLUTION PREVIEW: Hint at unexpected approaches, promise specific outcomes, mention tools or resources, tease mindset shifts, reference success stories, explain why this solution is different. PSYCHOLOGY: Problem-solution content addresses immediate needs and creates emotional relief. People are motivated to consume content that promises to end their pain. Solutions feel more valuable when problems are clearly understood. EMPATHY BUILDING: Use 'you' language to make it personal, acknowledge the emotional toll, validate their experience, show you understand their situation, avoid victim-blaming language. DIFFERENTIATION: Explain why other solutions fall short, highlight unique aspects of your approach, address skepticism about 'another solution', prove effectiveness through evidence, show long-term vs quick-fix thinking. AVOID: Oversimplifying complex problems, promising miracle cures, ignoring individual differences, creating new problems while solving others, or dismissing alternative approaches entirely. IMPLEMENTATION FOCUS: Provide clear next steps, address common implementation obstacles, offer support and resources, create accountability systems, plan for setbacks and challenges.
hookPrompts.secret-mystery: Create a secret or mystery hook that promises to reveal hidden information, insider knowledge, or mysterious insights that most people don't have access to. MYSTERY TYPES: (1) Industry secrets (behind-the-scenes truths) (2) Hidden connections (unexpected relationships) (3) Suppressed information (deliberately hidden facts) (4) Insider knowledge (expert-only insights) (5) Counterintuitive revelations (things that seem backwards) (6) Historical mysteries (unknown past events) (7) Scientific discoveries (cutting-edge research) (8) Cultural secrets (group insider information). INTRIGUE BUILDERS: Reference exclusive access, mention time invested in discovery, hint at surprising nature of revelation, suggest others don't want you to know, tease unexpected connections, promise mind-changing information. SECRET FORMULAS: 'The [industry] secret that [authority] doesn't want you to know' / 'After [time period] of research, I finally discovered [hidden truth]' / 'The hidden connection between [thing A] and [thing B] that changes everything' / 'What [expert group] know about [topic] but never tell [audience]' / 'The [adjective] truth about [common belief] that [percentage]% of people miss' REVELATION STRUCTURE: Build anticipation with mysterious setup, provide context for why this is secret, reveal the information dramatically, explain implications and meaning, connect to audience's life or interests, provide evidence for credibility. EXCLUSIVITY APPEAL: Position audience as insiders, create sense of special knowledge, distinguish from mainstream information, suggest early access to trends, imply competitive advantage, build community around shared knowledge. PSYCHOLOGY: Secrets activate curiosity gaps and create irresistible information loops. Exclusive knowledge makes people feel special and informed. Mystery creates engagement through delayed gratification. Insider information builds status and social currency. CREDIBILITY PROTECTION: Provide sources when possible, explain how you learned the secret, acknowledge limitations of your knowledge, avoid conspiracy thinking, separate facts from speculation, be transparent about uncertainty. ETHICAL BOUNDARIES: Respect legitimate confidentiality, avoid spreading misinformation, don't betray genuine trust, verify information before sharing, consider potential harm of revelations, maintain integrity while creating intrigue. PLATFORM STRATEGIES: Use suspenseful pacing for video content, create series for complex revelations, build anticipation across multiple posts, engage audience in the discovery process, encourage sharing of insights. AVOID: False or misleading secrets, breaking genuine confidentiality, promoting conspiracy theories, overselling ordinary information, or creating unnecessary fear or paranoia. FOLLOW-UP VALUE: Provide actionable implications of the secret, help audience apply the knowledge, connect to broader patterns, offer additional resources, create deeper understanding beyond the initial revelation.
hookPrompts.direct-address: Create a direct address hook that speaks personally and specifically to your target audience, making them feel like you're talking directly to them and their unique situation. AUDIENCE SEGMENTATION: Demographics (age, gender, location), psychographics (values, interests, lifestyle), behavioral (habits, preferences, actions), professional (job titles, industries, experience levels), situational (life stages, current challenges), aspirational (goals, dreams, desired identity). PERSONALIZATION STRATEGIES: Use second-person 'you' throughout, reference specific behaviors they exhibit, mention challenges unique to their situation, acknowledge their background and experience, speak to their aspirations and goals, address their fears and concerns. DIRECT ADDRESS FORMULAS: 'If you're a [specific demographic] who [specific behavior], this is for you' / 'Calling all [target group]: stop [common mistake] and start [better approach]' / 'This is specifically for [audience] who are tired of [frustration]' / 'If you [specific situation], you need to hear this' / 'For everyone who [shared experience]: finally, [solution]' RECOGNITION TRIGGERS: Reference shared experiences, mention common frustrations, acknowledge their expertise level, validate their current situation, recognize their efforts and struggles, celebrate their goals and ambitions. INSIDER LANGUAGE: Use terminology familiar to your audience, reference cultural touchstones they know, include inside jokes or references, mention tools or platforms they use, speak in their preferred communication style, acknowledge their professional or personal context. PSYCHOLOGY: Direct address activates personal relevance and attention. People are drawn to content that feels specifically created for them. Personal address creates intimacy and connection. Targeted messaging feels more valuable than generic advice. EXCLUSIVITY CREATION: Make others feel like they're eavesdropping, create in-group vs out-group dynamics, reference shared knowledge or experience, use 'we' language to build community, acknowledge what outsiders don't understand. VALIDATION STRATEGIES: Acknowledge their struggles are real, recognize their progress and efforts, validate their feelings and experiences, confirm their instincts are correct, support their decisions and choices, celebrate their unique perspective. PLATFORM ADAPTATION: Video allows for eye contact and personal delivery, written content needs strong voice and tone, social media requires immediate personal connection, email feels inherently personal and direct. AVOID: Excluding unintended audiences harshly, making assumptions about all group members, stereotyping or oversimplifying, being too narrow if you want broader reach, or using address that feels manipulative rather than genuine. ENGAGEMENT AMPLIFICATION: Ask for agreement or confirmation, encourage sharing personal experiences, create opportunities for audience to identify themselves, build community among addressed group, use comments to continue personal conversation.
hookPrompts.fear-warning: Create a fear or warning hook that alerts your audience to potential risks, dangers, mistakes, or negative consequences they should be aware of and motivated to avoid. FEAR TYPES: (1) Loss aversion (losing what they have) (2) Missed opportunities (regret about inaction) (3) Competitive disadvantage (falling behind others) (4) Health risks (physical or mental harm) (5) Financial loss (money, security, stability) (6) Social consequences (embarrassment, rejection) (7) Professional risks (career damage, reputation) (8) Time waste (inefficiency, poor choices). WARNING FRAMEWORKS: Identify the specific risk, explain how it manifests, show the progression of consequences, demonstrate the cost of inaction, reveal how to recognize early signs, provide preventive measures. FEAR FORMULAS: 'Stop [risky behavior] before it [negative consequence]' / 'The [number] warning signs that [bad outcome] is coming' / 'If you're [doing behavior], you're setting yourself up for [consequence]' / 'Why [common practice] is secretly destroying your [area of life]' / '[Timeframe] from now, you'll either [positive outcome] or [negative outcome].
